data.body:
[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with THIS child (S2 70c7: Content-addressed snapshot cache + background janitor)]

Ran the 7 cross-child interaction modes for this cache+janitor child against the WHOLE sibling roster (S1 6d56 materialize core, S3 fa49 gate threading, S4 30b3 attested signing, S5 b7fa CLI/MCP surfacing, S6 1ba5 docs). All 7 modes are clean under the high-confidence / benign-reading filter. EMPTY G4 findings list for this pairing.

Mode-by-mode:

1. Implicit shared state — CLEAN. The cache on-disk layout is a shared-state contract split coherently between this child and S1, with each producer/consumer relationship mutually named:
   - The atomic dir-rename POPULATION primitive is OWNED by S1 ("Atomic population: build into <root>/tmp/<uuid>/ ... rename() the directory into the cache path"; S1 Scope "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)"). This child CONSUMES it ("Cache entries keyed by SHA, populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"; this child Scope "Out: materialization itself (S1)"). No double-implementation of population.
   - The tmp/ residue + startup cleanup contract is consistent across the seam: S1 AC3 ("a crashed materialization leaves only a tmp/ dir, cleaned on startup"); THIS child OWNS that cleanup ("Startup sweep: rmtree tmp/* trash/*; reconcile byte-total with one full walk"). Producer-of-residue (S1) → sweeper (S2) agree on the tmp/ location and the startup-cleanup responsibility.
   - This child PRODUCES the cache sub-layout it owns (locks/<sha>, gc/lock, trash/<uuid>, the byte-total) and consumes the flock+atomic-mkdir-fallback portability seam ESTABLISHED by S1 — its cross-process flock(LOCK_EX) on locks/<sha> and the gc/lock RIDE that seam without re-owning the tar/tmp/fallback sub-clauses. No shared-state value is produced-without-consumer or consumed-without-producer.

2. Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN. This child's reader-safety model (delete-on-last-close: readers open() up front; eviction under an open fd is harmless; ENOENT/EIO/short-read → treat as miss → re-materialize) MATCHES S3's consumer-side contract exactly (S3 AC6: "a gate tolerates an absent/being-GC'd snapshot dir by re-materializing (ENOENT → miss), never reading a partial tree"). The content-addressed "immutable SHA is a perfect cache key (no staleness)" assumption matches S1's resolve-ref→immutable-SHA and S4's reproducibility-over-identical-content claim. This child assumes only materialized-SHA entries enter the cache, which is consistent with S3's "Local: ... no materialization, no cache" (local bypasses this child entirely). No contradiction with any sibling. (The standing S1↔S3 local-mode seam — whether materialize() takes a source_mode param — lives on S1/S3 and does not touch this cache child: this child never claims a local path.)

3. Dependency gap — CLEAN at this child. Every upstream this child depends on is present and named: the atomic dir-rename / faithful materialization it caches is S1 (6d56, named in this child's What + Scope). Every downstream that depends on this child is present: S3 consumes the cache via its ENOENT-tolerant re-materialize path; S4 signs over the SHA that keys this child's entries; S6 documents the disk-cap behavior (its AC5). The ADR this child authors ("ADR: capture the cache architecture ... AND the rejected PID+heartbeat lease") is cross-referenced by S3 ("the architecture ADR (authored in S2, cross-referenced here)") — the producer (S2) and the cross-referencer (S3) agree on authorship. No unowned hand-off originates here. (The standing S3↔S4 attest.py snapshot-handle routing seam is downstream of S1's resolved-SHA output on the signing side, not this cache child's concern.)

4. Scope overlap — CLEAN. The cache/janitor vs materialization boundary is bilaterally scoped-out (S1 "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)" ↔ S2 "Out: materialization itself (S1)"), so the atomic-population primitive is implemented once (S1) and merely consumed here. Parent AC4 splits cleanly with no double-ownership: S1 owns atomic-population + no-worktree + no index.lock/config.lock contention; THIS child owns single-flight (AC1) + delete-on-last-close (AC2). Parent AC5 (cache + janitor reclaim under pressure without disrupting in-flight reads) is owned solely here. The ADR is authored once (here), documented-as-prose elsewhere is not claimed (S6's AC4 is the HMAC trust-model doc, not the cache ADR) — no docs/ADR double-ownership. The disk-cap KNOB docs are S6 (its AC5); the disk-cap BEHAVIOR is this child — clean behavior/docs split. No overlap.

5. Ordering violation — CLEAN. The encoded dependency graph (S1 blocks {S2 70c7, S3 fa49}) makes this child blocked-by S1, which is COHERENT with the producer→consumer flow: the materialize core (S1, which owns the atomic dir-rename + tmp/ residue contract) precedes the cache that populates from it. The edge points the benign way; no contradiction. Parent AC8 only requires all-closed + green.

6. Consumer impact — CLEAN. This child's contracts (single-flight; delete-on-last-close reader safety; ENOENT/EIO/short-read → miss → re-materialize; touch-on-read mtime LRU; rename-to-trash eviction; gc/lock interlock; configurable tunables) are consumed without redefinition by S3 (whose AC6 ENOENT-tolerance is precisely the gate-side complement of this child's delete-on-last-close model) and rest atop S1's population. No downstream contract is broken by this child's interface; the reader model this child builds is exactly what S3's gate-side tolerance assumes.

7. Residual references — CLEAN. All cross-refs resolve: S1's atomic dir-rename (real producer in roster), the rejected PID+heartbeat lease (the spike outcome cited in the parent's Context, captured in this child's ADR), shutil.disk_usage / flock(LOCK_EX) / relatime-noatime (real OS primitives), and the "verdict memoization (deferred fast-follow)" scope-out (matches the parent's Out-of-scope "Verdict memoization (sha,gate)->verdict — fast-follow"). The disk-cap docs hand-off to S6 (its AC5) resolves. No dangling reference.

Note: the cache/janitor modules are pre-manifest (roster-wide search returns no repo_snapshot/cache implementation in the tree), so the review is grounded in the ticket-spec wording of the S1↔S2 population seam and the S2↔S3 reader-tolerance seam, both of which are mutually consistent.

Conclusion: no high-confidence cross-child inconsistency between this cache+janitor child (S2) and the parent/siblings. Its single-flight, delete-on-last-close, byte-total-integrity, trash-drain, gc-lock-interlock, startup-sweep, and ADR deliverables form clean consumer/producer splits with S1 (population→cache), S3 (cache→ENOENT-tolerant gate read), S4 (SHA→signing key), and S6 (disk-cap→docs). The standing roster seams (S1↔S3 local-mode source_mode; S3↔S4 attest.py snapshot-handle routing) live on other children and do not touch this pairing. EMPTY G4 findings list for this pairing.
